video_title: The Future of Onchain Collateral | Misha Putiatin
gist: Misha Putiatin, co-founder of Symbiotic, reframes the protocol from a restaking/shared-security project into a permissionless 'collateral markets' framework (Core V2) aimed at making tokenized real-world assets liquid and composable in DeFi. He argues the RWA opportunity is larger than the infrastructure/shared-security one, that the industry has only proven value in treasuries so far, and that the real 'dream' is moving actual businesses onchain rather than just tokenizing offchain receipts.

themes.theme: Symbiotic's pivot from restaking to collateral markets

insights.claim: Symbiotic no longer defines itself as a restaking or shared-security protocol; Putiatin now positions it as a 'collateral markets' framework spanning both infrastructure staking and financial collateral.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest, Symbiotic co-founder)
insights.so_what: Reassess Symbiotic against RWA/collateral-infrastructure comparables rather than restaking peers like EigenLayer; the addressable market it is targeting is different.
insights.quote: we call ourselves a collateral markets to create framework for a location that is secure enough and predictable enough

insights.claim: Putiatin claims the collateral/finance opportunity is materially larger than the shared-security infrastructure market Symbiotic originally served, which is why it rebuilt its stack around finance.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Signals where restaking-era infrastructure teams may migrate for TAM; watch for capital rotating from pure infra security to onchain collateral rails.
insights.quote: the opportunity in in finance is actually way bigger than what we have in in infrastructure

insights.claim: Before founding Symbiotic, Putiatin and his co-founder ran audit/security firm State Mind, which he says audited and secured roughly $22 billion of TVL across onchain protocols.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Establishes security-first credibility of the team; relevant when underwriting smart-contract risk of Symbiotic's collateral framework.
insights.quote: we ended up auditing like and securing around 22 billion dollars of TVL

insights.claim: Putiatin frames today's DeFi as pre-Henry-Ford car manufacturing where every protocol and blockchain is 'homemade,' making risk research, scoring, and capital-deployment decisions nearly impossible without shared frameworks.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: The bull case for standardized collateral frameworks: standardization lowers per-protocol diligence cost and unlocks larger institutional capital.
insights.quote: we were in the era uh pre Henry Ford cars car manufacturing right so every car is unique

insights.claim: Symbiotic spent roughly the last six-to-eight months building Core V2, a single capital-allocation framework designed to serve a wider range of financial applications than the infrastructure-focused Core V1.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Core V2 is the product to track for adoption; its launch cadence and adapter count are the near-term signal of traction.
insights.quote: the last like six eight months we were developing corv2 that caters to a wider range of applications

insights.claim: Core V2's design goal is that committed collateral 'remains productive between obligation events'—a vault can allocate to multiple DeFi or Symbiotic apps and simultaneously withdraw and unwrap to serve liquidity, so there is no point in time where capital is idle.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Capital efficiency is the pitch to LPs; the mechanism competes with idle collateral sitting in withdrawal queues, potentially raising blended yields on RWA collateral.
insights.quote: there is no no uh point in time where it's idle

insights.claim: Core V1's infrastructure-era design imposed hardcoded delays (e.g., a 14-day slashing wait) even when a lender and creditor agreed funds should be returned, because it was built for decentralized-network security rather than finance.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Illustrates why V2 was necessary; friction in V1 limited financial use cases, so V2 removes a real adoption blocker.
insights.quote: even if everyone agrees that the money needs to be returned before the slashing period part passes I symbiotic just consider is it like somebody's attacked

insights.claim: Despite not being designed for finance, Symbiotic's Core V1 attracted about $300 million of delegation to a single financial app, which Putiatin cites as evidence of latent demand ahead of V2.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: A concrete demand data point; V2 removing V1's friction could scale this figure materially if the thesis holds.
insights.quote: we got like 300 million of dollars of delegation to a financial app

themes.theme: Liquid Lane and the RWA liquidity unlock

insights.claim: Liquid Lane lets holders of real-world assets exit early—before RWA terms of 90 to 180 days elapse—by matching them with LPs who buy the position at a small discount instead of waiting in a redemption queue.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Early-redemption liquidity is the wedge product; discount spreads become a tradable, LP-earnable yield source on locked RWA collateral.
insights.quote: before the term. So some RWA are like 90 to 180 days. You need to wait. You go into withdrawal queue

insights.claim: Putiatin argues long-duration RWAs face a chicken-and-egg problem—size is hindered by lack of redemption liquidity, while deposit flow is hindered because they aren't big or widely used in DeFi—and that making the liquidity provision market-priced breaks the loop.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Explains why long-duration tokenized credit has stalled; a market-based liquidity layer is the specific unlock to watch for RWA scaling.
insights.quote: the the RWA size, especially the long duration one is hindered by the lack of liquidity

insights.claim: Symbiotic uses Redstone's liquidation framework, routed through Liquid Lane, to handle liquidations of RWA collateral positions.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Names an oracle/liquidation dependency; Redstone is a downstream beneficiary of RWA liquidation volume in this stack.
insights.quote: we work with Redstone um Redstone for they have their own liquidation uh liquidation framework and they're going to be going through us

insights.claim: Putiatin lays out a three-step RWA onboarding lifecycle: (1) sell holders liquidity, (2) provide 24/7 pricing via perps/secondaries that can seed an oracle while offchain markets are closed, and (3) deeper DeFi composability.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: A staged roadmap for how tokenized assets actually become DeFi-usable; useful for timing which RWA infrastructure bets mature first.
insights.quote: selling them defy is like the step to the first step is selling them liquidity

insights.claim: Putiatin says that outside of treasuries and some speculative assets, the industry has not yet proven it can deliver real value from tokenized RWAs, and that the asset class remains 'in its infancy.'
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: A sober check against RWA hype; suggests treasuries are the only proven category and other tokenized assets carry unproven adoption risk.
insights.quote: except of treasuries and maybe like some speculative assets uh we are we have not proven that we can like actually deliver a lot of value

insights.claim: Putiatin notes there are already three or four completely different methodologies for how tokenized stocks are structured under the hood, and it is unresolved which approach will win.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Standards fragmentation in tokenized equities is a live risk; backing the wrong tokenization methodology could strand liquidity.
insights.quote: even for like tokenized stocks we have like three or four methodologies that are completely different in terms of how what what how it's done under the hood

insights.claim: Putiatin argues the real payoff for tokenized assets is composability—looping, using them as collateral, and CDP/lending markets—which he says will create a durable 'base yield' analogous to how Ethereum staking once anchored stablecoin rates.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: If RWA collateral becomes the new base-yield anchor, it reshapes DeFi rate curves and stablecoin economics; watch RWA-collateralized lending markets.
insights.quote: the the the actual fun starts when they are composable

insights.claim: Putiatin frames composability as a 'three-prong attack': an auditable framework, a liquidation/liquidity layer (Liquid Lane), accurate net-asset-value reporting, plus insurance—arguing you cannot use an asset as collateral unless it can first be liquidated.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Defines the exact prerequisites (liquidation + NAV + insurance) for RWAs to become DeFi collateral—a checklist for evaluating any RWA-collateral protocol.
insights.quote: once you have accurate net asset value reporting and once you have like the third the third strike is like once you have an insurance for people that really need that uh like from compliance standpoint

insights.claim: Putiatin expects gated (KYC-perimeter) and open/permissionless RWA rails to coexist rather than merge, because merging would require blockchains and protocols to be 'unhackable and unabusable,' which he says is not achievable.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Implies a permanent two-tier RWA market; permissionless venues (e.g., Maple-style non-gated RWAs) remain a distinct, more adventurous segment.
insights.quote: they're going to be like always safer and more adventurous parts

insights.claim: In Core V2, each curator opts into specific asset types and conditions—e.g., one curates only private credit, another only money-market funds, another only insurance—so 'shared' collateral is not homogeneous and outcomes hinge on curator quality.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Curator selection is the key underwriting variable for LPs; risk is concentrated in individual curators' analysis, not diffused across a homogeneous pool.
insights.quote: each curator opt in into assets with the conditions uh that like they are comfortable with

insights.claim: Putiatin says the front-and-center risk for RWA collateral is not black swans but net-asset-value repricings that trigger avoidable (potentially cascading) liquidations when the asset is used in DeFi; instant liquidity lets holders borrow or deliver a little and avoid forced losses.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Reframes the main RWA collateral risk as NAV-repricing liquidation cascades; instant-liquidity backstops are the mitigation to watch.
insights.quote: there was a reprisal of net asset value and it was like not significant one but because it was actually used in DeFi somewhere people fell into risk of liquidation and like and maybe even cascading liquidation

insights.claim: Putiatin stresses that tokenized RWAs are treated as actual securities in many jurisdictions (not an ambiguous 'is Bitcoin a security' case), forcing Symbiotic to engage local lawyers in each jurisdiction such as Bermuda, the Caymans, or BVI.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Cross-jurisdiction legal overhead is a real cost and moat; RWA protocols with mature multi-jurisdiction compliance have a structural advantage.
insights.quote: those are like well in in a lot of jurisdictions those are considered to be actual securities

insights.claim: Putiatin identifies non-portable KYC as a major friction: completing KYC at Centrifuge does not carry over to Securitize or a third platform, and he argues reusable zkKYC would be a huge onboarding unlock.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Portable/zk identity is a specific gap; a protocol solving reusable KYC across RWA venues could capture broad onboarding demand.
insights.quote: once I went through KYC in centrifuge I don't have KYC in securityize

insights.claim: Putiatin cites a paradigm-related protocol he refers to as '3J' that was using or planning to use ZK proofs for credit scores and KYC, but questions whether such approaches remain legal.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Points to early zk-identity experiments but flags legal uncertainty; regulatory risk, not tech, is the binding constraint on zkKYC.
insights.quote: 3J I think is using that their paradigm pure and friendly protocol. uh they are using ZK I guess for or at least they were planning to use that for credit scores for KYC. Yeah, not sure it's if it's even legal anymore

insights.claim: Symbiotic's near-term roadmap is to 'populate' the Core V2 framework, with Cap, Nexus, and Liquid Lane as the first trio of apps, existing adapters for Morpho and Aave, and delta-neutral strategies plus more private credit next in the pipeline.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Concrete integration list to track adoption; adapter count and app launches are the measurable near-term milestones.
insights.quote: cap, nexus and liquid lane would be the first trio among others

insights.claim: Putiatin states Liquid Lane is about to launch its first asset class very soon with Fasanara Global, KPK, and Barter as the first wave of issuers/partners.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: A specific imminent catalyst with named launch partners; the first live asset class is the proof point for the liquidity thesis.
insights.quote: liquid lane is about to launch I would not say it but like really really soon for the first asset class with Fasanarium Global and KPKard and Barter

insights.claim: Putiatin's stated 24-month goal is to build a system where capital has no incentive to ever leave—secure within the framework yet flexible enough that LPs don't miss out on opportunities elsewhere.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Frames sticky TVL as the multi-year success metric; retention/stickiness of collateral is how to judge whether the framework is winning.
insights.quote: the dream basically on like 24 months I guess is to create a place where capital does don't never had has incentive to ever leave

insights.claim: Putiatin's contrarian take is that the real 'sellout' is abandoning the mission by merely tokenizing offchain receipts; he argues the dream is moving actual businesses onchain, citing Cap as a partner whose credit is originated and converted into stablecoins onchain rather than issuing a mere receipt token.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Distinguishes 'ticket tokenization' from native onchain businesses; use it as a lens to separate superficial RWA plays from ones with genuine onchain economics.
insights.quote: bringing some token like some receipt on on something that's like somewhere offchain and and just like taking these tickets and exchanging them. This is not the dream

insights.claim: Putiatin's personal contrarian view is that self-custody was never the central point of crypto for him—the smart-contract programmability was always the front-and-center value.
insights.speaker: Misha Putiatin (guest)
insights.so_what: Downplays self-custody as the crypto value driver, aligning with institution-friendly custody models; a tell for how RWA-native builders prioritize composability over sovereignty.
insights.quote: my contrarian thinks that like self- custody was not front and center of it, right? the the smart contract of it part of it was always front and center at least for me
